---
title: "The Scurvy Story"
subtitle: "How we lost and rediscovered the cure, told in three causal models."
format:
  html: default
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: true
---

## The story

Scurvy devastated sailors on long voyages: weakness, bleeding gums, and death. In **1747**, James Lind showed that **lemons** prevented scurvy, a correct finding, but the *reason* was unknown. Later, people decided it was **acid** killing the "bacteria" that caused scurvy. That wrong belief led to replacing lemons with limes or vinegar, and scurvy returned. Only in **1928** was the true cause found: **vitamin C** (ascorbic acid). The three directed acyclic graphs below show how the assumed data-generating process changed over time.

## DAG 1: 1747. Lemons prevent scurvy (correct but incomplete)

Lind's experiment showed that lemons prevented scurvy. The causal view was simply: lemons → scurvy prevention. Correct as far as it went, but the mechanism was missing.

```{python}
#| label: dag-1-1747
#| fig-cap: "1747: Lemons prevent scurvy (correct but incomplete)"

import daft
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pgm = daft.PGM(dpi=180, alternate_style="outer")
pgm.add_node("lemons", "Lemons", 1, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#fef9e7", "edgecolor": "#b8860b", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_node("sp1", "Scurvy\nPrevention", 3.5, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#d5f5e3", "edgecolor": "#1e7e34", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_edge("lemons", "sp1", plot_params={"head_length": 0.3, "head_width": 0.12, "linewidth": 2, "edgecolor": "#333"})
plt.title("1747: Lemons prevent scurvy (correct but incomplete)", fontsize=11, pad=10)
pgm.render()
plt.show()
```

## DAG 2: Misguided belief. Acid kills bacteria (wrong)

People came to believe acid killed the bacteria that caused scurvy. The assumed chain was: acid → bacteria death → scurvy prevention. That misunderstanding led to dropping lemons for cheaper acids and brought scurvy back.

```{python}
#| label: dag-2-misguided
#| fig-cap: "Misguided belief: Acid kills bacteria (wrong)"

import daft
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pgm = daft.PGM(dpi=180, alternate_style="outer")
pgm.add_node("acid", "Acid", 1, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#e8e0f0", "edgecolor": "#4a148c", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_node("bd", "Bacteria\nDeath", 3.5, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#fce4ec", "edgecolor": "#c2185b", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_node("sp2", "Scurvy\nPrevention", 6, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#d5f5e3", "edgecolor": "#1e7e34", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_edge("acid", "bd", plot_params={"head_length": 0.3, "head_width": 0.12, "linewidth": 2, "edgecolor": "#333"})
pgm.add_edge("bd", "sp2", plot_params={"head_length": 0.3, "head_width": 0.12, "linewidth": 2, "edgecolor": "#333"})
plt.title("Misguided belief: Acid kills bacteria (wrong)", fontsize=11, pad=10)
pgm.render()
plt.show()
```

## DAG 3: 1928. Vitamin C prevents scurvy (complete and correct)

In 1928 the real mechanism was identified: **vitamin C** prevents scurvy. That explains why lemons worked (they contain vitamin C) and why the acid-only story failed.

```{python}
#| label: dag-3-1928
#| fig-cap: "1928: Vitamin C prevents scurvy (complete and correct)"

import daft
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

pgm = daft.PGM(dpi=180, alternate_style="outer")
pgm.add_node("vc", "Vitamin C", 1, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#e0f7fa", "edgecolor": "#006064", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_node("sp3", "Scurvy\nPrevention", 3.5, 1, aspect=3.8, scale=1.2,
             plot_params={"facecolor": "#d5f5e3", "edgecolor": "#1e7e34", "linewidth": 2.5, "alpha": 0.95})
pgm.add_edge("vc", "sp3", plot_params={"head_length": 0.3, "head_width": 0.12, "linewidth": 2, "edgecolor": "#333"})
plt.title("1928: Vitamin C prevents scurvy (complete and correct)", fontsize=11, pad=10)
pgm.render()
plt.show()
```
